metadata.summary: DORA metrics measure delivery performance: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time, Change Failure Rate, MTTR. Elite teams deploy 208x faster, recover 2,604x quicker. Add Reliability metric.

content.title: DORA Metrics
content.summary: DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics are four research-validated KPIs that measure software delivery performance: Deployment Frequency, Lead Time for Changes, Change Failure Rate, and Mean Time to Restore. Elite teams deploy 208x more often and recover 2,604x faster than low performers. A fifth metric — Reliability (SLO achievement) — was added in 2025. AI-generated code reduces delivery stability by approximately 7%, so batch size must be monitored when using AI tools.

why.p: The four metrics are the only empirically-proven predictors of both technical and organizational performance (from the "Accelerate" book, 2018). They give a complete picture: throughput (Deployment Frequency + Lead Time) and stability (Change Failure Rate + Mean Time to Restore). Without them, teams optimize locally and miss systemic bottlenecks.

Elite performers are 2x more likely to exceed organizational performance goals. They achieve high scores on all four metrics simultaneously, proving that speed and stability are not trade-offs but correlated.

list.item: Engineering org wants a baseline + improvement plan for software delivery performance.
Quarterly/annual exec reporting: leadership needs a credible answer to "are we shipping faster?".
Tracking AI-assistant adoption impact (DORA 2025 research shows AI affects stability/throughput; need data).
Comparing teams' delivery health without falling into "lines of code" or "story points" anti-metrics.
Justifying platform/CI investments — DORA metrics provide before/after evidence.
Setting up engineering metrics for a team or service for the first time.
Evaluating CI/CD pipeline health before a refactoring sprint.
Connecting engineering output to business outcomes (revenue, churn, team wellbeing).
Running quarterly DevOps performance reviews.
Designing a metrics collection system backed by CI/CD + incident tools.
Single-person / very small teams — the variance dominates the signal.
As a stack-rank tool ("Team X is Elite, Team Y is Low — fire the manager"). Goodhart's Law applies hard.
For non-software work (data pipelines, ML training, content) — definitions don't transfer cleanly.
When the org doesn't yet have basic CI/CD — measure something else first (build pass rate, deploy automation %).
As the only health metric — DORA + Reliability + SPACE + developer experience surveys complement each other.
Comparing individual developers — metrics are team-level, not individual.
Early-stage prototype projects with no production — no deployment signal to measure.
When incident tooling (PagerDuty, Opsgenie) is not yet integrated — MTTR will be inaccurate.

section.name: four-key-metrics
section.p: DORA metrics measure two dimensions of delivery health:

rule.statement: Measure all four DORA metrics as a set — throughput (Deployment Frequency + Lead Time) and stability (Change Failure Rate + MTTR) together reveal the full delivery health picture.
rule.rationale: Optimizing only throughput metrics drives risky large batches; only stability metrics drives slow but safe delivery. Elite performers achieve high scores on all four simultaneously.

list.item: Deployment Frequency: Elite = multiple/day; High = weekly–monthly; Medium = monthly–6mo; Low = 6mo+
Lead Time for Changes: Elite = <1 hour; High = 1 day–1 week; Medium = 1–6 months; Low = 6mo+
Change Failure Rate: Elite = 0–15%; High = 16–30%; Medium = 31–45%; Low = 46%+
Mean Time to Restore: Elite = <1 hour; High = <1 day; Medium = 1 day–1 week; Low = 1 week–1 month

rule.statement: Track trends, not absolute values — week-over-week improvement matters more than hitting exact elite thresholds.
rule.rationale: Teams in different domains (embedded, banking) have structural constraints that prevent elite Deployment Frequency; improvement trajectory predicts organizational outcomes better than absolute tier.

section.name: metric-definitions-and-data-sources
section.term: Count of successful production deployments per time period. Data: CI/CD pipeline events, deployment logs.
Time from first commit to successful production deployment (merge timestamp to deploy timestamp). Data: Git commit timestamps + CI/CD deployment timestamps.
Percentage of deployments requiring rollback, hotfix, or causing an incident. Data: incident tracking system + deployment records.
Average time from incident detection to resolution. Data: incident management (PagerDuty, Opsgenie) resolved timestamps.

section.name: business-impact
section.p: Research findings from Accelerate (Forsgren, Humble, Kim, 2018):

list.item: Elite performers are 2x more likely to exceed organizational performance goals.
Elite vs low: 208x higher deployment frequency.
Elite vs low: 106x faster lead time.
Elite vs low: 2,604x faster recovery time.

rule.statement: Add Reliability (SLO achievement rate) as a fifth metric alongside the four core DORA metrics.
rule.rationale: DORA 2025 research shows SLO achievement and error budget consumption predict organizational outcomes independently of the four delivery metrics.

rule.statement: Monitor batch size when using AI code generation tools; expect a ~7% reduction in delivery stability with heavy AI adoption.
rule.rationale: AI-generated code encourages larger commits and pull requests, which violates the small-batch principle underlying elite DORA performance.

section.name: data-collection-rules
section.p: Accurate data collection is critical for valid metrics:

rule.statement: Automate metric collection from CI/CD pipelines — never rely on manual reporting.
rule.rationale: Manual reporting introduces lag and bias; teams under pressure stop updating it. Automated collection from deployment events is the only reliable baseline.

rule.statement: Define "production deployment" precisely before collecting data: successful deploy to prod environment, not staging.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous definition inflates Deployment Frequency when staging deployments are counted. The signal must reflect real user impact.

rule.statement: Mark deployments as rollback or hotfix at emit time, not retroactively, so Change Failure Rate is accurate.
rule.rationale: Retroactive classification is inconsistent across team members; the CI/CD pipeline knows at deploy time whether the trigger was a hotfix branch or rollback workflow.

rule.statement: Link incident records to the deployment that caused them; Mean Time to Restore without deployment linkage cannot distinguish release-caused incidents from infrastructure failures.
rule.rationale: Only release-caused incidents contribute to Change Failure Rate. Infrastructure-only incidents belong in reliability/SLO tracking, not Change Failure Rate.

list.item: GitLab native: enable DORA analytics (Premium/Ultimate tier) — zero additional tooling.
GitHub + any incident tool: Google Four Keys (open source, GCP-native event pipeline).
Multi-platform enterprise: Sleuth, LinearB, or Jellyfish for aggregation.
Custom stack: Prometheus + PostgreSQL schema + Grafana dashboard (templates in this methodology).

rule.statement: Export deployment events to a Prometheus-compatible endpoint or PostgreSQL from every CI/CD pipeline immediately after each successful deploy.
rule.rationale: Post-hoc scraping of CI/CD logs is fragile; emit events at the point of deployment where all metadata (commit SHA, timestamp, is_rollback) is available.

section.name: process-integration
section.p: Using DORA metrics appropriately within organizations:

rule.statement: Review DORA metrics in team retrospectives, never in performance reviews of individual engineers.
rule.rationale: Using DORA metrics to evaluate individuals causes gaming (smaller commits, avoided deployments, suppressed incident reports) that destroys the signal.

rule.statement: Set weekly automated reports; alert on degradation (not on absolute low values) during the first 90 days of collection.
rule.rationale: First 90 days establishes baseline. Early alerting on low absolute values discourages teams before the baseline is known.

section.name: deployment-event-emission
section.p: Example implementations for emitting deployment events:

example.title: GitHub Actions deployment event emission
example.code: - name: Record Deployment Event if: success() run: | curl -X POST "$METRICS_ENDPOINT/events/deployment" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer ${{ secrets.DORA_API_KEY }}" \ -d '{ "service": "${{ github.repository }}", "environment": "production", "commit_sha": "${{ github.sha }}", "commit_timestamp": "${{ github.event.head_commit.timestamp }}", "deployed_at": "'$(date -Iseconds)'", "is_rollback": false, "is_hotfix": false }'
example.note: Emit deployment event AFTER successful deploy, not before.

example.title: Recording deployment at pipeline start — wrong placement
example.code: - name: Record Deployment (wrong placement) run: | curl -X POST "$METRICS_ENDPOINT/events/deployment" ... ./deploy.sh # deploy happens AFTER recording — wrong
example.note: Will emit even if deploy fails, inflating Deployment Frequency.

section.name: promql-queries
section.p: Prometheus queries for DORA metrics calculation:

example.title: Deployment Frequency: deployments per day over last 7 days
Change Failure Rate as percentage over 30 days
MTTR in minutes (average incident resolution time)

section.name: lead-time-calculation
section.p: Python implementation for lead time statistics:

example.title: Lead time calculation from deployment records

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Agents should follow a structured approach to DORA implementation:

DORA metrics are continuous-collection problems with periodic reporting. Have a "collector" agent ingest from Git (commits, PRs), CI/CD (pipeline runs, deploy events), and incident systems (PagerDuty/Opsgenie/Statuspage) into a normalized event store. A "computer" agent runs daily to recompute the four metrics + reliability per service per team. A "narrator" agent (Opus) produces the weekly summary: which metric moved, why (correlate with deploys, incidents, batch size), and what to investigate. Always show trends over absolute numbers; always include batch-size and AI-adoption signals as confounders. Never let one agent both compute and adjust definitions — separate.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives definition specs and dashboard PRs through quality gates.
A custom dora-event-normalizer (Sonnet) — given heterogeneous events, maps to {deploy, change, incident, recovery} schema with a confidence score; flags low-confidence rows for human review.
A custom dora-narrator (Opus, read-only) — given weekly metric snapshot + diff vs prior, produces a 200-word narrative with hypothesized drivers; cites supporting evidence.

section.prompt:
Compute DORA metrics for service , period

. Inputs: git events (commits with merge_at), deploy events (pipeline `deploy_prod` success), incident events (severity, started_at, restored_at, deploy_correlation), feature-flag events. Output: (1) Deployment Frequency (deploys/period), (2) Lead Time (p50, p95: merge_at → deploy_prod), (3) Change Failure Rate (incidents-tied-to-deploy / deploys), (4) MTTR (incident_started → restored), (5) Reliability proxy (SLO attainment %), (6) batch size (commits/deploy), (7) AI-assist signal (% PRs with AI co-author tag). Forbid: counting non-prod deploys, inventing definitions, mixing services without disclosure, hiding small-N caveats (period < 10 deploys → mark as "low confidence").

Narrate week-over-week change. JSON: {metric, prev, curr, delta, hypothesized_drivers:[{driver, evidence_event_id, confidence}], suggested_investigation, confounders:[batch_size, ai_adoption, incident_severity_mix]}.

section.name: common-failure-modes
section.p: Known pitfalls in DORA metric implementation:

antipattern.description: "Deployment" definition is the swamp: deploys to prod-like staging? Feature flag rollouts? Per-service or per-monorepo?
antipattern.reason: Without a written, agent-readable definition, every team measures something different.

antipattern.description: Lead Time conflates "commit to merge" + "merge to deploy" + "deploy to release"; agents collapse them and miss the actual bottleneck.
antipattern.reason: Measure merge-to-deploy specifically, not from commit.

antipattern.description: Change Failure Rate depends on incident classification — many "rollbacks" are routine config changes, many incidents aren't tied to a deploy.
antipattern.reason: Agents inflate or deflate CFR depending on the incident-system schema.

antipattern.description: MTTR includes detection time + response time + recovery time; agents compute from incident.opened_at → resolved_at and miss the silent half-hour before alert fires.
antipattern.reason: Use incident detection time, not created time.

antipattern.description: 2025 research: AI-assistant adoption correlates with -1.5% throughput, -7.2% stability — likely because AI encourages larger batches.
antipattern.reason: Agents must track batch size or DORA improvements are illusory.

antipattern.description: Vendor-tool dashboards (Sleuth/LinearB/Jellyfish) have opinionated definitions that may not match yours; agents take their numbers at face value.
Elite-vs-Low buckets feel motivating but encourage gaming (artificial micro-deploys to bump frequency).
"Reliability" as the fifth metric is not yet standardized — implementations vary; agents should expose error budget burn or SLO attainment, not invent their own.
antipattern.reason: Use SLO attainment as the reliable proxy.

antipattern.description: LLMs invent thresholds ("Elite means daily deploys") — anchor every claim to a citation; the official thresholds change yearly in the State of DevOps report.
Lead-time computation often pulls pr.created_at instead of pr.merged_at; this inflates lead time and hides queue-after-merge time.
antipattern.reason: Use merge timestamp, not creation timestamp.

antipattern.description: Agents include hotfix deploys when computing Deployment Frequency (good) but exclude them from Change Failure Rate (wrong) because they don't open an incident — fix the schema, not the calc.
antipattern.reason: Include hotfixes in all metrics consistently.

antipattern.description: Multi-service repos: agents attribute deploys to the wrong service when the deploy event lacks a service label.
antipattern.reason: Force the deploy event to carry {service, sha, env, success}.

antipattern.description: AI-coauthored PRs are detectable via co-author trailer or commit-message tags; agents skip the signal and miss the AI-impact dimension.
antipattern.reason: Expose AI adoption signal in reports.

antipattern.description: "Time-zone aware" matters: deploy at 23:00 UTC vs 06:00 UTC affects Deployment Frequency buckets at month boundaries; pick UTC and stay there.
Agents recompute history retroactively after a definition tweak — breaks trust.
antipattern.reason: Version the definitions and freeze old data; show "definition v2 from 2026-01-15".

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Proven practices for successful DORA implementation:

list.item: Write a definitions doc in the repo (docs/dora-definitions.md) that humans + agents both read; treat it as an Architecture Decision Record.
Track per-service, not per-team — service ownership shifts; service identity is stable.
Show trends with confidence intervals or low-N flags; one bad week shouldn't trigger reorgs.
Pair Deployment Frequency with batch size (commits per deploy). Frequency without small batches is vanity.
Include the 5th metric (Reliability) as SLO attainment, not invented uptime number.
Annotate dashboards with deploys, incidents, AI-tool rollouts, team changes — narrative explains the curve.
Use DORA for retrospectives, not performance reviews. People game what's used to judge them.
Re-baseline after major org/process changes — comparing to old baseline misleads.
Connect DORA to business outcomes (revenue, NPS, churn) once trust in definitions is established.

template.purpose: GitHub Actions workflow step emitting deployment events to a metrics endpoint.
Prometheus recording rules and alerts for all four DORA metrics.
PostgreSQL schema for deployments + incidents tables with DORA materialized view.
